text: An eight millimeter port is inserted in the right midclavicular line for a fenestrated bipolar grasper, with two left-sided ports for monopolar electrocautery and a prograsp forceps. A right-sided twelve millimeter assistant port is placed for suctioning and needle passage. The surgeon mobilizes the stomach, creating a window in the gastrocolic ligament to access the lesser sac. The stomach is sutured to the anterior abdominal wall for retraction. The pancreas is identified, appearing grossly abnormal, and intraoperative ultrasound is used to visualize the pancreatic duct and stones.

The surgeon uses monopolar electrocautery to open the pancreatic duct along its length, revealing several stones. These stones are carefully removed, starting from the distal end towards the tail and progressing proximally.

An ultrasound is used to locate the gastrogluteal artery in the neck of the pancreas. A window is created in the transverse mesentery to bring the Roux limb to the lesser sac. The proximal jejunum is identified at the ligament of Treitz and brought to the mesenteric window. The mesentery is divided using a robotic vessel sealer, and the jejunum is divided using a robotic stapler.

The surgeon performs a side-to-side enteroenterostomy by placing two anchoring sutures proximal and distal to the planned anastomosis site. Two enterotomies are created using electrocautery, followed by the formation of a side-to-side stapled enteroenterostomy. The resulting enterotomy from the staple is closed with barbed monofilament absorbable suture.

The surgeon brings the Roux limb through the mesenteric defect, positioning it adjacent to the pancreas. An enterotomy is created in the jejunum, matching its length to the opening of the pancreatic duct. A longitudinal pancreatic vaginostomy is formed using monofilament absorbable suture, starting at the tail apex. The posterior portion of the anastomosis is sutured first, ensuring precise approximation between the jejunal serosa and the pancreatic cut edge. The anterior layer is sutured similarly, with careful inversion of the mucosal edges for optimal approximation.

Near the completion of the anastomosis, the surgeon ensures the lengths match to prevent a dog ear deformity at the corner. The pancreatic jejunostomy is sprayed with fibrin sealant, and the stomach is released from retraction.
